landmarks.name: Clocking Out
landmarks.script: Alright - you're off the clock, Everett-bound, and honestly: congratulations. We'll slip out of South Lake Union and get you onto I-5 heading north in just a second. The evening move: Amazon's towers shrinking in the mirror, the Space Needle waving goodbye, and about forty minutes of freeway between you and home. Get comfortable, drop your shoulders - let's point this thing north.

landmarks.name: Ship Canal & Lake Union
landmarks.script: Up and over the Ship Canal Bridge - same double-decker, opposite direction. This time Lake Union is over on your left, to the west: the houseboats, the little seaplanes dropping down to land right on the water, and Gas Works Park on the far shore. It is a genuinely great view to end a workday on. Soak it up before the freeway ducks back down between the hills.

landmarks.name: University of Washington
landmarks.script: That's the University of Washington sliding by on your right now. It's older than the state of Washington itself - founded back in 1861. Husky Stadium is the big one down by the water, where the die-hards literally sail up and tailgate from their boats. And in spring, the cherry blossoms in the Quad become a whole citywide event. Alright - onward, north.

landmarks.name: Green Lake
landmarks.script: Green Lake neighborhood off to your left - that little glacial lake with the famous loop path around it. Even now, end of the day, it'll be packed with runners, dog-walkers, and at least one guy on rollerblades who still refuses to accept that it is no longer 1994. One of the most-loved corners of the whole city. Noted - and rolling on.

landmarks.name: Northgate
landmarks.script: Northgate coming up - home of one of the very first true shopping malls in America, opened way back in 1950, the blueprint that basically every other mall in the country copied. These days there's a light-rail station, and - the fun part - the ice rink where the Seattle Kraken practice. Three sheets of NHL ice sitting where a shopping mall used to be. Peak Seattle, honestly.

landmarks.name: Jackson Park & Lake City
landmarks.script: Off to your right now, all those trees are Jackson Park, the big north-end golf course - and just past it runs Lake City Way, Seattle's gloriously scruffy old highway of taco trucks, dive bars, and vintage motels. You are heading back out toward the edge of the city. The suburbs are calling, and dinner is getting closer.

landmarks.name: Shoreline & Old Aurora
landmarks.script: On your left, running right alongside us, that's old Aurora Avenue - Highway 99 - the seedy, neon-lit original road north from back before I-5 ever existed. And you're rolling through Shoreline now, which is your cue: you are officially leaving Seattle proper and crossing back into suburb country.

landmarks.name: Back into Snohomish
landmarks.script: Right about here, you cross the line out of King County and back into Snohomish - your home county. This is officially the home stretch. From here it's a straight shot up the freeway: the trees get taller, the lots get bigger, and Everett is sitting there waiting at the end of it.

landmarks.name: Mountlake Terrace
landmarks.script: Mountlake Terrace - that quiet little in-between town that just landed its own light-rail stop, so it's busy quietly reinventing itself as a transit hub. Blink and it's already behind you. You're really cooking now.

landmarks.name: Edmonds Ferry
landmarks.script: Way off in the distance to your left - a good three miles west - is Edmonds and its ferry dock - the Edmonds to Kingston run straight across Puget Sound. If home can wait, that boat is basically a floating happy hour with a killer view. But let's assume you want your own couch tonight - onward we go.

landmarks.name: Lynnwood
landmarks.script: Lynnwood - Alderwood mall, and the northern end of that new light-rail line that finally stitched all of this to the city. It's also roughly where Interstate 405 folds back in from the Eastside. Not much farther now - you can practically smell home from here.

landmarks.name: Silver Lake & Mill Creek
landmarks.script: Silver Lake and Mill Creek on your right now - the big leafy bedroom-suburb belt. Roundabouts, good schools, a 'town center' that is honestly a very nice strip mall, and a whole lot of people who drive precisely this stretch home every single evening. You are basically one of them now. And that's not a bad thing - nearly there.

landmarks.name: Boeing Everett Factory
landmarks.script: And there it is again, way off in the distance to your left - a few miles west - the Boeing Everett Factory, the single largest building on the entire planet by volume. It's so big that clouds once formed up near the ceiling before they fixed the airflow. Every 747, 777, and 787 rolled out of that one building. Your hometown builds the biggest airplanes in the world - not a bad thing to be coming home to.

landmarks.name: Snohomish River Delta
landmarks.script: You're crossing the Snohomish River again - back out over that wide delta of sloughs and marshland, salmon runs, and a frankly absurd number of birds. On the far side, the freeway lifts you up and sets you down in Everett. Almost home now.

landmarks.name: Home in Everett
landmarks.script: And - you're home. Welcome back to Everett. The waterfront, the Navy ships, the whole working aerospace-town skyline - all yours again. That's the round trip, city and back, done and dusted. Nicely driven out there. Go put your feet up. Same guide, same road, first thing tomorrow morning.

landmarks.name: Alderwood & the 405 Split
landmarks.script: Alderwood mall off to the side here, and the big Interstate 405 interchange - if you'd been Eastside-bound for Bellevue or Redmond, you would have peeled off back there. But you're not; you're going home. Notice the trees getting taller and the sky opening up? That's Snohomish County welcoming you back.

landmarks.name: The Boeing Freeway
landmarks.script: That big interchange curling off to your left is State Route 526 - the Boeing Freeway - heading west toward the aircraft plant and Paine Field. Which means you are right on the doorstep of Everett now. Just about home; you can practically see your exit from here.
